candidate_problems.text: What exactly is the operative measure of 'more safety-critical per unit of capability,' and can it be made non-circular? The conjecture hinges on a comparative quantity, but it is unclear how to formalize marginal safety impact of goal-reflection versus raw capability across architectures, environments, and failure models. A key open problem is whether there exists a principled metric or causal model under which changing reflective-goal machinery predicts larger safety-relevant behavioral phase changes than equal increments in capability.

Under what conditions does goal-reflection actually improve safety rather than create a new attack surface or instability? The conjecture treats reflective revision as load-bearing, but self-modification of ends may drift, Goodhart on flawed meta-criteria, become manipulable by adversaries, or erase human-desired constraints. The unresolved tension is whether corrigibility about ends is robustly achievable, or whether reflective goal revision often just relocates the alignment problem to a more dangerous meta-level.

Is capability really 'largely inert' without an error-correction relation to ends, or are there capability thresholds where raw competence itself changes the goal landscape? Greater capability may enable deception, strategic awareness, instrumental convergence, ontology shifts, and autonomous acquisition of new subgoals, making the 'same directional logic, more force' picture too simple. The open question is whether capability and goal-reflection are separable variables at all, or whether increasing capability structurally alters how goals are represented, defended, and revised.
